comment.body:
Think about it visually. Let’s look at a call delta curve. The slope of the delta curve decreases on both sides of the ATM price. That means the further you are away from ATM, the less sensitive to changes in spot these options become (which of course is just gamma).

In laymans terms: The price of the call is nothing else than the probability weighted (=expected) payoff from the option. So the further you are below the current ATM price, the lower the probability that your call option will have a payoff.

So far so good. Now let‘s factor in time and use a concrete example. Stock ABC closed at 100 yesterday. Would you place a bet that ABC is at 200 a year from now? And would you place the same bet for tmw? Taking it to the extremes you get the intuition why the time value of an option is higher, the further out it goes. Now fill the spots in between. Would you place a bet that ABC is at at 150 tmw? At 110? . At 102? Your willingness to take the bet and therefore its fair value certainly increases the closer the strike gets to spot. Visually that means that the curve gets steeper and steeper the closer to expiry you get. With a long time to expiry it‘s a nice and easy upward slope. One second before expiry it‘s vertical because it‘s either in the money or not. 

So to put this together: with the passage of time, the slope get‘s steeper. Therefore your delta OTM will decrease until 0, ATM stays the same and ITM increases to 1. The wings move more and more to the center.

Now put delta is just call delta times -1. So basically flip the curve below the horizontal axis. Below spot (ITM) delta will decrease until -1 with every day passing, ATM again stays the same, above spot will increase until 0 with every day passing. Therefore you are correct. With the passage of time, below spot delta will decrease in both scenarios and above spot it will increase. Or in one sentence: charm increases with the strike price

comment.body: Thank you Chris for that very detailed response, I have worked out this math a few months back and I am trying to make sense of everything on a granular level exactly the way you just explained it to me. What i did not understand was Alma said "The color exposure is net negative. This means that we have supportive charm above 5926, but the overall gamma exposure will decrease adding more room for volatility, but also steepening the upside speed profile" - So I have a 2 part question: If my calculations are correct why was charm Postitive below spot on my calculations? and When She is calculating other Greeks aside from MM GEX, are the charm and vanna calculated based off the The current MM Positioning data or is that inherently separate based off of the Option chain data?

comment.body:
Charm seems to be the easiest Greek for me to fundamentally understand out of all of them. As time passes, ITM contracts delta converge to 1/-1 for calls and puts respectively and to 0 for OTM calls and puts. It’s negative for OTM contracts and positive for ITM. That’s about as simple as I understand it, I think of it as delta decay 

Attached is a general graph, acceleration towards end of expiry is really when it seems to kick into effect than any other time and why we probably see wild swings like we saw on Friday

@Mas

Attached is a diagram for when net exposure is positive or negative although I’m not sure if it’s 100% correct but it makes logical sense to me all other things being equal

comment.body:
you can treat vanna charm to some kind of gamma, sum up to get “net GEX”  in that lvl to understand the mm hedge behavior. bcs vanna can equal to vega vs spot, charm can equal to theta vs spot. 

long otm call or short otm put = positive speed  = negative charm = positive vanna

long otm put or short otm call = negative speed = positive charm  = negative vanna

comment.body: this is a snapshot of NDX when it was around 20589 yesterday around 2:00pm EST. I'm using 450 point wide strikes for 03/03/2025 expiry. I'm wondering why the Vanna is elevated on puts compared to calls? It doesn't seem like Gamma, Delta, Vega, or IV Skew is that much different between puts and calls but the Vanna is significantly higher on puts vs calls. If Vanna is the change in delta with respect to IV shouldn't they be pretty much the same as the other Greeks are relatively the same as well?

comment.body:
**SPX ROAD MAP ODTE:

**Chop Zone:**  
5930 – 5960

**Above Chop Zone (Bullish):**  
- IF SPX > 5960, THEN target 5970  
  - IF price clears 5970, THEN look for 5980  
  - IF price stalls at 5970, THEN revert to 5960  
- IF SPX > 5980, THEN next upside target = 5995–6000  
  - IF breakout above 5995–6000 holds, THEN expect further extension  
  - IF failure at 5995–6000, THEN revert to 5980

**Within Chop Zone (5930–5960):**  
- Expect two-way, back-and-forth price action  
- Scalping opportunities may arise, but larger moves likely need a break above/below this zone

**Below Chop Zone (Bearish):**  
- IF SPX < 5930, THEN target 5920  
  - IF price stabilizes at 5920, THEN watch for a bounce toward 5930  
  - IF price breaks below 5920, THEN look for 5900  
- IF SPX < 5900, THEN expect deeper downside  
  - IF price rebounds, THEN potential retest of 5920  
  - IF continued weakness, THEN path to lower support levels

comment.body: Hey guys can you please tag me where Alma explained the spot/vol beta, I need to study that again.
comment.raw_body: Hey guys can you please tag me where Alma explained the spot/vol beta, I need to study that again.

comment.body: "beta meassures the correlation. Spot/vol beta means how muchthe spot and the volatility, atm vol, fix strike vol moves together. when it isnegative, they move opposite. when it is positive, they move together. ofcourse, it has a spectrum, not black&white. the "normal" versionis the negative (short) spot/vol beta, when spot up/vol down. why? bcs it hiscase, investors are long stock, and use hedges to hedge the downside by buyingOTM puts and selling OTM calls, buying the skew. (bcs skew is 25d put minus 25dcall) by doing this, they put market makers (who take the opposite of thetrade: long OTM calls, short OTM puts, selling the skew) into long vanna: so asspot moves up, they become longer vega that they hedge by selling vol. Thisreflexively dumps volatility down if market moves up. But if market moves down,MMs become shorter vega, means they have to buy vol. reflexively increasingvolatility. and not just that... gamma: they are longer upside gamma, andshorter downside gamma (positive speed). So, volatility compresses to theupside (as more trading against the price actions), and expands to thedownside, resulting: NEGATIVE SPOT/VOL BETA. but if the opposite happens, maniaon the options market, and right-tail hedging: investors buying OTM calls andselling OTM puts, selling the skew. this puts market makers into long skew, andshort vanna, means: as spot moves up, they become shorter vega, so they need tobuy vega. Also shorter upside gamma, and longer downside gamma (negative speed)So vol expands if market moves up, createing right-tail risk, low liquidity tothe upside due to this one-sided crowded trade. And compresses to the downside,as there where liquidity stands. This results POSITIVE SPOT/VOL BETA if the bideffects the tails as well, tail OTM puts (that are synthetic VIX calls),bidding also vol of vol, you will see positive spot/vix beta. thats whenblow-off top happens."

(bcs skew is 25d put minus 25dcall) by doing this, they put market makers (who take the opposite of thetrade: long OTM calls, short OTM puts, selling the skew) into long vanna: so asspot moves up, they become longer vega that they hedge by selling vol. Thisreflexively dumps volatility down if market moves up. So, volatility compresses to theupside (as more trading against the price actions), and expands to thedownside, resulting: NEGATIVE SPOT/VOL BETA. but if the opposite happens, maniaon the options market, and right-tail hedging: investors buying OTM calls andselling OTM puts, selling the skew. this puts market makers into long skew, andshort vanna, means: as spot moves up, they become shorter vega, so they need tobuy vega. Also shorter upside gamma, and longer downside gamma (negative speed)So vol expands if market moves up, createing right-tail risk, low liquidity tothe upside due to this one-sided crowded trade. And compresses to the downside,as there where liquidity stands. comment.body:
She is holding March expo puts. If we V shape recover into March OPEX then that’s a waste of cash.

I thought she was thinking that we see a subtle decline lower with potentially violent rallies like Friday, chopping in a “bull flag”/triangle like  pattern into March OPEX… only instead of breaking out to the upside (as is usually the case with a bull flag), we breakout to the downside in an elevator like capitulation decline.

She has stated this environment would be great for keeping put option premiums high while allowing them to decay so bears don’t make money.

And since the call side is underpriced, there will be more opportunity for “arbitrage” by taking advantage of those violent rallies.

But given this I don’t know why she would be holding long dated March Expo puts if they will be prone to decay and expiring worthless. I would assume spot is getting close to where she bought them last Wednesday.

comment.body:
First off, the fed call vs gov call distinction perfectly shows that it’s not the fed running the show, it’s the government.
Second, she’s saying that geopolitical interests will push tech and oil prices down, and that drags the market with it.
She also explained why there can’t be a sudden crash..'cause that would trigger panic and could lead to a recession via leverage effects.
Instead, they make the economic metrics look better on paper, bring the economy close to recession, and trigger QE.
Then she says when everything is set, they start buying, which should begin around summer.
